sentences.text: No, Auschwitz was not a work camp.
It was strictly a processing camp and an exterminating camp.
At least, that's-- that I know.
Auschwitz, to me, was strictly a matter of being processed and being taken out to the next phase of my journey, which would be--
No, I don't.
I could not identify it.
I did not go back to Auschwitz.
And I only know I was in block four.
Whatever that would be, I was in block four.
I do know Auschwitz was a humendously big place.
So wherever block four was is where I-- where my block was.
Well, I realized that almost from the inception.
Because those were the rumors, those were what people were saying is that-- well, I should made a correction to that-- that they would have to survive on their own.
It would not be a-- they would have to do what I was doing.
And, by the grace of God, or luck, or whatever it took, they would have to do their own way of surviving.
And so did I think I was going to see my mother again?
Every moment, every minute of my life, until it was confirmed to me that she was dead.
She was murdered with my sister.
Is that when I had hopes that, like me, she, too, and my sister would survive.
So the answer to your question is I always hoped that she would survive, and that I would be able to be with her again.
You were starting to say before about beginning to leave Auschwitz.
What caused you to leave?
The reason we left Auschwitz is a very awkward time, was like 2, 3 o'clock in the morning, is because the Russian front was-- we were already hearing some artillery firing, distant artillery firing.
And there was some rejoicing, at least by the elders, that there was hopefully some kind of a relief coming, possibly.
But in any event, we were herded out of the barracks at-- late-- late at night.
I suppose it was past midnight.
I know it was dark, very dark.
Think it was around September, late September in '44.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were-- we were marched for a long time to-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] to some alley.
We did not get our numbers, because we were evacuated at a very fast pace.
And we were part of a-- of a transport that never got numbers.
We were never stamped with-- tattooed with numbers.
And we were going through this particular area and section, and-- where we saw women standing on the right side when we were marching, walking, hollering out to us names.
Schleimer, Ben, is that you?
Is that you?
They were hoping to hear maybe the answer of a loved one, or friend, or child that was being transported out, and that to-- they wanted to confirm or identify that they were still alive.
So all we heard was these names being thrown at us.

And we, in turn, was hollering out, Schprensa, Hankel, Mama, whatever we could say to be able to hopefully say, oh, then, we didn't hear that, we said, [NON-ENGLISH] did you know if this one survived.
You know, while we were walking.
This is while we're walking.
These women were all lined up at these barbed wires, and they were half-naked and just-- just-- you know, it was dark.
I mean, it was dark.
This is still in the camp where we were walking towards the trains, towards the transports.
And I don't know, my dad was still with me at the time.
And he says that he thought he saw my mother and my sister because-- I don't know that to be a fact, but he claims that that's the last time he thought he saw them.
And he hollered out to them that we were alive.
And I like to think that was good, because it was a positive, cause that would then give them more courage to try to go on, you know.
But I don't know that.
And we continued on and we got on these trains, on these wagons again.
Cars, these cattle cars, box cars.
And we were loaded on those.

And I remember when they pulled out of Auschwitz, we were going right through the station in Vienna because I was looking through these cracks there-- of these wooden cracks, and I saw the-- you know how a train station is with the signs.
We were driving by.
I know it was Vienna.
And we were passing through.

And then when we came through the outskirts somewhere, maybe 20 minutes, a half hour out, on the other side of Vienna, or Austria, whatever that was, or however we were going, they were looking to separate this transport.
They were sending some people to one camp, and others to another camp.

Because that was-- this is how they would distribute the work force to wherever workers were needed, is when we were requisitioned by some of these factories, by some of these places that needed labor.
So when the train stopped, and we all had to disembark again.
And when we disembarked, they gave us some bread, and then they told us start loading up again.
It was another condition of chaos and confusion.
And I got on this train, and then, when this wagon was full and loaded, my dad wasn't able to get on the same wagon.
And they threw him on the next wagon, on the other wagon, on the other cart.
And so we got separated.
And he always told me that I should always save myself piece of bread for later on, because I would never know if I wouldn't eat.
So when I got my piece of bread, I took two bites of it and I went in the wagon.
When I got in the wagon, I got beat up pretty badly, and they took my bread away from me, the other people.
Well, the other-- what am I going to call them?
Prisoners?
Inmates.
I admit I always relate inmates to crime inmates.
So whoever we were, one of those, OK, no, two of those.
And they stripped me of my bread and beat me up pretty good.
And when the train came to the next stop, I couldn't move.
So the-- one of the Germans comes in, and he sees me laying there.
And believe it or not, he goes out and he makes a issue with the elders.
He says, this is what you do to your own.
And beat 'em up, and so on.
And I learned lesson number two.
You get your bread, you eat it.
There's not going to be another time for you to eat it.
So it was a lesson well-- if you want to call it such.
That's just another point of survival.
You just-- there is no next time, and there is no next minute, and there is now.
And that's when you eat it, and that's when you go on.
If you have an opportunity to get someone else's bread, you do so, OK?
It's just a part of a process, unfortunately.
And this is how I started to learn to survive.
Not to take anybody else's, I was too little.
But even as a young boy, as a young kid, Germans would like to see us fight.
In other words, they would instigate a fight.
And they would tell you that the winner would get some bread.
So we would fight like animals.
Not-- we just got into a fight.
There was one in this place called Kaufbeuren, where they-- this was part of their entertainment.
They asked us to-- they'd give us a loaf of bread of the winner.
So we did this a few times, and then, ultimately, we got together and we said, OK, we'll do it for a couple of minutes, and then, you know, one of us will go down, and then we'll split the bread.
And then the fight was no longer an exciting thing to them, so they stopped doing it.
You just-- you find ways to adjust to the situation.
This is what you do to one of your young children.
And, of course, no one's going to answer.
It was like the next day.
It was like the next day, because I don't-- I don't know how long these-- once you're in one of these boxcars, it's dark, and you're just in your own world.
And you hear these-- these wheels on these tracks, da-da-da, da-da-da.
You know, that's all you hear, and you-- time doesn't mean anything.
If you leave at night, you get there in the morning, maybe it's four hours later, or maybe it's a day later.
I tell you, I was not able to coordinate what was-- what time of day or night it was.
But I would say it was either the next day or maybe several hours later.
And so I got off the train, and we got into this camp called Kaufbeuren.
And there, I met my next experience as to get beat up pretty good by a Kappo.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He gave an order.
And, you know, in a concentration camp, under-- under-- when they tell you to move, you better understand.
If not, you're out of luck.
And this guy is screaming all kinds of things, and people are moving, and I'm standing.
I don't know-- you know, I just didn't relate to what he was saying, and he just hit me with some kind of a rubber or something.
Of course, their favorite things were cow tails.
They were burnt cow tails with knobs on them.
So when you got whipped, it was like-- it was the-- the tail that was hardened with these-- with these knots on them, OK.
And he hit me on my head, and I just completely got knocked out.
And I don't know when I-- I don't know when I came to, but when I came to, I was not the same.
I was never the same, I can tell you that.
I don't know, maybe-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I just could not think very well anymore at that particular juncture.
And it took me a long time to just come around.
No, to me, it seemed like a long-- I was just having these headaches, and I was moving slowly, and I don't know.
It just-- I have to attribute it to that particular time.
And then they assigned me to-- in Kaufbeuren, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they assigned me to a detail up in the-- a kitchen detail.
There was 10 of us, 12 of us.
And there was a kitchen-- a German kitchen a few kilometers from the camp.
This camp was a fairly new camp in Bavaria.
And we had to go to this camp, to this kitchen.
This was our job, is to go to this kitchen, peel potatoes, and carrots, and cabbages, and so on and so forth, all day long.
And this was-- this kitchen was cooking for the German soldiers, and so on.
Then we would get the potato peelings.
And they would make, from the potato peelings, we would get the soups and so on for our camp.
And there's a little humor and tragedy.
This was a part of humor, because they assigned to us a older German soldier from the kaiser.
The man had to be in his, maybe, 70s.
And he wore those long bixes, the bixes, the long rifle.

You know, those long, heavy rifle, and he had to wear this long trench, the German coats, with the ammunition, and his canteen, and the rest of the paraphernalia, the rest of the military garb that he had to wear.
And this poor man could not-- when I say poor man, he was the home guard.
And because the young guys in the camps were always run by the-- by the SS, or somebody like that.
But they were SS who were either injured, or they lost a leg, or an arm, or an eye.
And since they could no longer be on the front line, their reward, or whatever it was, was to make them camp commanders.
And they were very, very angry.
They were very obviously angry people by losing one of their limbs, and so on.
So I can tell you that they were not nice guys, and they didn't-- did their job beautifully.
But this guy was an old guy, and so he had to guard us.
We'd leave the camp like about 6:00 in the morning, and we had to go through all these corn fields.
It seemed like it was kilometers after kilometers, until we got to the kitchen.
And when we got to the kitchen, we peeled all these things, and this guy had to march us all the way back in the evening.
And he would sit all day long, kibbutz with the Germans while we were peeling potatoes all day long, and cabbage, and so on.
And this went on for about two or three weeks.
And, finally, the last kid in the end, he would say he kind of liked us, you know, because what else could he do?
And he couldn't schlep his rifle anymore, he couldn't drag this, because it was just a load on him.
So he would take off the minute we got into these corn fields, in this area that was not visible.

He would give-- he would take the ammunition out of the rifle and he'd give the last guy to carry his rifle, and one guy would carry his belt, and the other one was that, until we got about maybe half a kilometer before the kitchen, and he'd put his garb back on.
This went on for a long time.
So, consequently, he sort of became lax on his guard, and we became very alert.

And what was happening, when we got back to the camp at night, the elders would say, while you guys are peeling the potatoes, and you're doing all the thing, why don't you cut some potatoes in half, and carrots in half, so we can get better food and more nourishment?
So we went back to the kitchen, and we started a system.
You know, we would cut the potatoes, and this and that, and so on.
We got away with it for a couple of weeks.
Then we got a little greedy, because hunger is a very, very, very, very hurting thing.
And so we started to bring food back.
We would just put strings around our waist, and on the bottom of our cuffs of the-- of these burlap uniforms we had.
And we would load those up with carrots, and cabbages, and potatoes, and so on.
And since this was in the fall in Bavaria, so it gets dark.
And the old man, he knew what we were doing, but he, at that point, was very kind of lax.
And we kept bringing this stuff in.
And we gave it to the-- you know, we gave it our elders.
Because we ate as much as we wanted to in those kitchens.
It was, to us, like a mitzvah, it was like the greatest thing that ever happened in the world to us.
Anyway, someone-- one of the guards saw us one night.
One night we come in, and all the lights are on in the camp.
This is about 7:00 at night.
And the camp commander and all the Kappos are all out there.
And they have everybody out there, everybody, in the camp at an appell.
And they take us up to these tables and they tell us to unload.
And we unload all the-- this, this, this, these vegetables that we had brought there.
And, again, this guy makes a speech that we entrusted these young Jews with an important this, and so on, and can't be trusted, and so on, so forth.
And this is the thanks, and we unloaded the stuff, and he's going to give us each 25 whips a piece, which was not out of the ordinary for the camp's punishment.
There was like a table in the middle of the thing.
And that's where they tied you down and they whipped you.
So there was one kid, kid's name was Schleimer, and he was a young-- he was very smaller kid, and so-- and we said, he isn't going to make it.
You know, we were-- and we're not trying-- I'm not trying to say here that we were trying to be heroes.
But we asked the Kappo if one of us could take one or two of his, so that he-- you know, this kid could have never made it.
And the camp commander said no, and we all got whipped 25 lashes for that.
And he got whipped, and the kid died.
He died about a week later, or whatever.
He bled to death.
That was the end of the kitchen routine with us.
And we were there for maybe another two or three weeks.
And we were sent out to another camp.
Kaufbeuren, I was probably about six weeks.
Six weeks in Kaufbeuren.
And then, from there, we went to Augsburg.
Augsburg, I was-- this was a work-- another work camp.
We went on these trucks that-- that were-- I remember that that were gassed or fueled by-- they had these tanks on the side of the cab.
And they would put like shavings, wood shavings, and so on, and so forth.
And so I remember that, because we had to stop every once in a while and get these shavings and sort of knock them back in, and continue on in these trucks.
And we got to this camp in these trucks.
We got into this Bavarian forest.
It was these huge trees.
And when we got into this place, it was like totally, completely covered with trees.
And when we finally got to the facility, it was a Messerschmitt facility, it was an airplane facility, where they were building airplanes.
And it was completely camouflaged, and I was doing-- I was wiring something, in a cockpit, and the people that worked with me, or I work with, were French.
I don't know if they were French prisoners, or whatever, or if they were cooperating with the Germans, or were some sort of engineers.
But they were very nice to me.
And they would bring me some-- an egg once in a while, and so on, so forth.
Of all the camps, that was probably one of the nicest camps that I was in, which was a work camp.
And that's where they wanted us to perform best, because it was an important function for them, also.
And one day, we come in, we thought this would be a great place to finish the war or just stay there.
And then, one day, we were working on some detail and doing some kind of a ditch work or whatever, I never forget it, and when we got back, it was just totally destroyed.
The Allies must have-- but the incredible part of it was that they bombed it when we were not there.
So whatever their intelligence-- I mean, to this day, I'd like to believe that they did this knowingly that we were not in that factory.
Because they did it in such a fast and furious fashion that when we got back, the factory was completely annihilated.
And so when we got back, there was nothing any more to work for us.
And so we continued on to another camp.
Augsburg, I was another four weeks, maybe five weeks.
These were just stopover camps.
Then I continued on to other little camps, which were created for strictly for work camps to do some specific things.
I even saw these factory, whoever they were, bosses that would take us and bring us back to these places that we like, we did road work, cut trees, and that kind of thing.
And one camp, I remember, I lived underground.
It was-- it was built under-- half of the building was underground.
It was warm, which was warm.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then, I went-- Then we started marching towards the Dachau.
Yeah.
We marched.
There was no more-- there was no more trains after that.
After, there was no more trains.
All the trains apparently were utilized for the troops, and they needed all those.
So we no longer-- and, plus, the marching eliminated a lot of us.
By the virtue of eliminating us, if that was the final solution, then the march achieved its-- and because we were just dropping like flies.
If you look back on our march, you would see nothing but bodies as far as your eye can reach and see.
So we went.
We marched to-- well, you know, when you talk marching from one place to another, you're talking of marching for a week.
OK?
And we would march through some of the cities, believe it or not, and people would virtually throw things at us.
Now, we would-- had to march through because there was no other way for us to go.
Most of our marching was done in, like, little farmlands and little communities that you went through.
And this one city, I don't remember the city, but we would come through, and women and men were just throwing things at us.
I mean, you know, like, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] like we were the scum of the Earth.
And that stood out in my mind.
You know, so when anybody-- I'm not condemning the entire German race, because, like, I'll give you some examples.
That there was the human element in it, as there is everywhere else.
And I'm not condemning, again, the entire German people.
But this crap of not saying that they knew about what was going on is not a true thing.
Because at the very end of the war, at the very end of this experience, we were exposed.
There was no longer hiding us.
There was no way of covering it up.
We were right out there.
We had to march.
We had to do-- in fact, as we walked, the dead people had to be picked up by the farmers.
This was an order by the Germans, that they couldn't leave the bodies.

So each area, each, like, community, or each farm section that we went through, these farmers had to pick them up in these wagons, horse wagons, put the bodies on, and somehow expose of them or eliminate them.
So everybody knew it.
There wasn't a matter of hiding it.
So that one particular scene with people throwing things, and throwing dirty Jew, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [GERMAN], and all that kind of thing was just there.
And again, the child situation.
You see no humanity, you see no compassion, you see nothing.
You just couldn't-- this thing just continues on, as there is no-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] nothing-- nothing and people to give you some indication there is some heart, some heart there somewhere.
And when we got to Dachau, again, this was a camp of-- not as big as Auschwitz was.
But that, too, was a processing camp.
And in that camp, I did see some prisoners from-- soldier prisoners.
There were captured prisoners.
I think there were some Americans, and British, and so on.
And the camp was very, very, very loaded, full with people.
And we were just hanging around that camp.
And then--
I got to Dachau in '45.
In '45, I would say around April or May of '45.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And, I tell you, the-- the Bavarian-- the Bavarian winters are just horrendously cold.
I mean, windy, and the only clothes we had was this burlap uniform, was made out of burlap paper, with the little hat.
And the shoes were made of-- the Holland shoes, they were wooden shoes.
And there was nails around those, and those nails would sometimes just come loose or something.
And your feet, it just-- you just could not survive.
It just-- that's why I'm looking today, I says, how did-- how did I survive?
And some of these other people?
I mean, I know not everybody experienced my experience, and I didn't experience a lot of things that-- the horrible things that happened to other people.

But when you march under these conditions, and you stay in forests under these conditions, and you are under-- under-- you know, if you go out right now, half-naked in this weather right now, you stay out there for two, or three, or four hours, and not come in with some sort of a pneumonia or some sickness, it would be a very lucky experience for you.
It would be a miracle that you don't get sick.
So I say to you, I don't know how we survived that.
I don't understand.
Forget about the hunger, I'm talking about the natural elements.
The very thing that kills people, is a miracle on its own.
And so when I go into April with you, you know, I'm going from September, and I go through the four weeks here, and the six weeks there, I'm not saying this in a very light way.

I'm saying that these were the hardest times, not just by being brutalized and being-- being under the-- under the conditions of being a kid in a concentration camp, but the elements, the elements were as horrendous as the whole concept of the concentration camps were.
So getting into April was a miracle by itself.
